coredata.dc:description: Many medical and chemical applications require the precise supply of antimicrobial components in a controlled manner at the location of mature biofilm deposits. This work reports a facile strategy to fabricate nanoscale metal-organic frameworks (NMOFs) coencapsulating the antibacterial ligand (lysine carbon dots, Lys-CDs) and targeted drug (folic acid, FA) in one pot to improve antibiofilm efficiency against established biofilms. The resulting products are characterized by transmission electron microscopy, field-emission scanning electron microscopy, powder x-ray diffraction, and ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy. The results show that Lys-CDs could coordinate with Zn2+ and the adding of FA inhibits the coordination of Lys-CDs with central ions of Zn. The Lys-CDs and FA are successfully exposed with the NMOFs disintegrating in the acid environment of bacterial metabolites. We are surprised to find a sharp increase of reactive oxygen species (ROS) inside the bacterial cells by FA functionalizing NMOFs, which undoubtedly enhance the antibacterial and antibiofilm activity. The as-synthesized ZIF-8-based nanocomposites also show the peroxidase-like activity in an acid environment, and produce extremely active hydroxyl radicals resulting in the improved antibacterial and antibiofilm activity. The possible mechanisms of antibacterial activities indicate that the presence of FA is significant in the sense of targeting bacteria. This study shows a novel approach to construct acid stimulation supply system which may be helpful for the research of antibiofilms.

head.ce:title: Acid-activated ROS generator with folic acid targeting for bacterial biofilm elimination

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Many medical and chemical applications require the precise supply of antimicrobial components in a controlled manner at the location of mature biofilm deposits. This work reports a facile strategy to fabricate nanoscale metal-organic frameworks (NMOFs) coencapsulating the antibacterial ligand (lysine carbon dots, Lys-CDs) and targeted drug (folic acid, FA) in one pot to improve antibiofilm efficiency against established biofilms. The resulting products are characterized by transmission electron microscopy, field-emission scanning electron microscopy, powder x-ray diffraction, and ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy. The results show that Lys-CDs could coordinate with Zn2+ and the adding of FA inhibits the coordination of Lys-CDs with central ions of Zn. The Lys-CDs and FA are successfully exposed with the NMOFs disintegrating in the acid environment of bacterial metabolites. We are surprised to find a sharp increase of reactive oxygen species (ROS) inside the bacterial cells by FA functionalizing NMOFs, which undoubtedly enhance the antibacterial and antibiofilm activity. The as-synthesized ZIF-8-based nanocomposites also show the peroxidase-like activity in an acid environment, and produce extremely active hydroxyl radicals resulting in the improved antibacterial and antibiofilm activity. The possible mechanisms of antibacterial activities indicate that the presence of FA is significant in the sense of targeting bacteria. This study shows a novel approach to construct acid stimulation supply system which may be helpful for the research of antibiofilms.

ce:section.ce:para: Pathogenic bacteria have brought inconvenience to people's lives. Especially in eutrophic environments, bacteria tend to accumulate leading the formation of biofilms [1]. It is well known that most of the difficult-to-kill bacterial colonies are associated in the formation of bacterial biofilms, which makes the bacterial resistant to antibiotics as well as disinfectant chemicals [2,3]. Obviously, it's difficult to completely remove biofilms with traditional antimicrobial treatments and unpredictable effects may arise while increasing the dosage of germicides [4,5]. Accordingly, great efforts are devoted to find new “weapons” to effectively remove biofilms while avoiding the creation of super bacteria [6,7].

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) play an important role in the body's defense against pathogen invasion [8,9]. Increasing the level of ROS at the infection sites is one of the effective strategies for sterilizating and disrupting the biofilm. It has been confirmed that the ROS not only degrades the biofilm extracellular polymeric substances with dispersing robust biofilms into single cells or smaller aggregates but also exhibits bactericidal effects with no record of stimulating resistance among bacterial strains [10]. Based on this strategy, a series of nanomaterials that can increase the level of ROS at the infection sites have been developed [11–13]. In particular, the construction of stimuli-responsive antibacterial nanostructures, which would target to infection sites without special conditions and disintegrate to generate large amounts of ROS only at the sites of infection, have attracted wide attention of scholars with the continuous development of nano antimicrobic material supply system [1,14–20].

Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) is a typical subclass of nanoscaled metal-organic frameworks (NMOFs), formed by coordination between zinc ions and 2-methylimidazole [21]. Specifically, ZIF-8 has excellent chemical stability under neutral conditions [22,23], and decompose rapidly in an acidic solution [23,24]. Thanks to the acidic microenvironment in bacterial accumulation [7,25] due to the anaerobic fermentation and the production of large amount of organic acid [10,26], ZIF-8 is suitable to be designed as an acid-responsive nanocarrier [27]. Han et al reported pH-responsive, light-triggered on-demand antibiotic release from functional ZIF-8, suggesting the therapeutic potential of ZIF-8-based smart platform for controlling biofilm formation [7]. Furthermore, ZIF-8 also exhibits inactivation activity against Escherichia coli. It has been confirmed that the photoinduced Zn2+ intermediate catalytic centers within porous NMOFs activate O2 to produce ROS [28]. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of carbon based nanomaterials, have attracted extensive interest due to their chemical stability and low toxicity [29], and gradually applied as bactericides with overcoming antibiotic resistance. In particular, some studies have demonstrated that nitrogen-doped CDs exhibit excellent antimicrobial activity [29,30]. The surface positively charge of CDs has been partially considered as the effect of disrupting the membrane integrity [31]. The other main factor is the generation of ROS, such as superoxide radicals and hydroxyl radicals [30,32,33]. In our previous work, we reported a nitrogen-doped CDs for simultaneously scavenging bacteria and promoting tissue repair [34]. Inspired by these features, we develop an acid-responsive ROS generator by combining Lys-CDs with ZIF-8 framework. Meanwhile, the ROS generation of the nanostructure and its role in antibacterial and antibiofilm mechanism are elucidated.

In addition, in order to further enhance the bactericidal effect and reduce the side effects of nanostructures, the drug molecule having a specific targeting effect is undoubtedly a double insurance. On the surface of bacterial cells inside biofilms, over-expressed folic acid receptors (FR) can recognize folic acid (FA) and promote the specific uptake of FA [35–37]. We speculate that, through combining the targeted FA with antibacterial nanosystem, we are increasing the specific uptake and reduce the outflow of antibacterial agents.

Herein, we develop a novel acid-responsive ROS generator for biofilm removal, which is constructed by simultaneous encapsulating Lys-CDs and FA into ZIF-8 smart platform with a facile one-pot approach. The morphology and intrinsic structure of ZIF-8 base nanocomposites are determined by SEM, XRD, UV–vis, FTIR and TGA. Then, we measure the disintegration of nanocomposites and expose behavior of Lys-CDs and FA under both normal matrix and simulated bacterial metabolic acid environment. The results show that ZIF-8-based nanostructure is a promising pH-responsive ROS generator for the removal of biofilms. The antibacterial tests show that ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA has significantly improved bacteriostatic rate against E. coli and S. aureus under acid conditions. While the nanostructure contacts with bacteria in vitro, we unexpectedly found a dramatic increase in the ROS level compared to the control groups. We also further measure the specific types of ROS. It is found that ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA exhibits hydrogen peroxide-like enzyme activity to produce superoxide radicals and hydroxyl radicals, which causes strong oxidative stress to destroy mature biofilms more effectively.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate, 2-methylimidazole and l-Lysine were purchased from Shanghai Meryer Chemical Technology Co. Ltd. (China). Folic acid (FA) was obtained from Nanjing WanQing Chemical Classware Instrument Co. Ltd. (China). 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, 99%), nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT, 98%), and terephthalic acid (TA, 99%) were obtained from Aladdin. The AO/EB Staining Kit was purchased from Molecular Probes (Eugene, OR, USA). Crystal violet (CV) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) kit was bought from Dojindo, Kumamoto. Ether, methanol and ethanol were acquired from Nanjing Middle East Chemical Classware Instrument Co. Ltd. (China) and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the zeolite imidazolate framework (ZIF-8)
ce:section.ce:para: ZIF-8 was prepared with minor modifications based on the previously described protocol [38]. Briefly, zinc nitrate hexahydrate (150 mg) and 2-methylimidazole (300 mg) were separately dissolved in 50 mL of methanol in two portions. Then, the 2-methylimidazole solution was added dropwise to the zinc nitrate solution. The reaction was kept magnetically stirred until a milky emulsion appeared followed by separation of products by centrifugation and washing with ethyl alcohol for several times. The white product ZIF-8 was obtained and freeze-dried for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of l-lysine carbon dot-encapsulated ZIF-8@Lys-CD
ce:section.ce:para: ZIF-8@Lys-CD was synthesized in a pot under mild conditions. First, Lys-CDs was synthesized according to a previous report [34]. Then, 50 mL the methanol solution of Lys-CDs (1 mg mL−1) was added dropwise to 50 mL the methanol solution of zinc nitrate hexahydrate (1 mg mL−1). Similarly, the 2-methylimidazole solution was slowly added dropwise to the above mixture solution while continuously stirring until the solution became a pale yellow emulsion. The obtained ZIF-8@Lys-CD was washed for 3 times with ethyl alcohol, and freeze-dried for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of folic acid and l-lysine carbon dot-coencapsulated ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, the methanol solution containing 50 mg of Lys-CDs and another methanol solution containing 30 mg of FA were added dropwise to methanol containing 150 mg of zinc nitrate hexahydrate successively. The methanol solution of 2-methylimidazole was added dropwise to the above solution. The reaction continued to stir until the solution became a yellow emulsion. Finally, the obtained ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA was washed 3 times with ethanol and freeze-dried for later use.

The sample (0.2 mg mL−1) was sonicated in deionized water. The morphological characteristics of the samples were measured by a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi) at 15 kV and the transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai12) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. In order to characterize the surface potential of samples and their particle size distribution in solution, the Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) tests were performed on a BI-90Plus zeta potential analyzer (Brook Haven). The Bruker D8 ADVANCE system (Bruker) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) in a range of 2θ from 20° to 80° was used to perform powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) to characterize the crystalline phases of samples. Fourier Infrared Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, Nicolet IS10) and UV–Visible Spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher, Evolution 220) were used to measure Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and ultraviolet-visible absorption (UV–vis), respectively. The thermal decomposition curves of the samples were measured by the thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, Mettler Toledo) with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. The laser confocal scanning microscope (CLSM, TCS SP8, Leica) was used to observe the survival rate of colonies inside the biofilm stained with Acridine Orange (AO) and Ethidium Bromide (EB). The viability of NIH-3 T3 stained cells with CCK-8 was observed by a multi-functional microplate reader at a wavelength of 450 nm (BioTek, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Decomposition of ZIF-8-based nanocomposites at different pH values
ce:section.ce:para:
Lys-CDs and FA expose assays were performed in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) at 37 °C. Briefly, 10 mg of ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA in PBS solutions (pH 5.5 and 7.4) were placed in 20 mL centrifuge tubes. The expose experiments were then performed at 37 °C under shaking of 100 rpm. Centrifuge and separately analyze the amount of products exposed after disintegration of ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA. Returning the sample to the original solution to maintain a constant volume, the cumulative expose percentage (CP%) of Lys-CDs or FA was then calculated,
CP % =
Mr Mtotal
× 100 %
Where Mr is the mass of Lys-CDs or FA exposed from the samples, and Mtotal is the total mass of loaded Lys-CDs or FA. The experiment was performed three times in parallel.

The antibacterial activities against E. coli (ATCC 25922, Gram-negative) and S. aureus (ATCC 25923, Gram-positive) were characterized by a minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC). The degree of damage to the free bacteria was observed by SEM. Bacteria were cultured in Luria-Bertani medium (LB) at 37 °C for 18 h. All of the utensils and samples were autoclaved prior to each antibacterial experiment.

Disperse all samples into LB broth to prepare the suspension of 1 mg mL−1. From the initial concentration of the samples, a series of 1 mL LB broth suspensions were prepared by two-fold dilutions. Then, 10 μL E. coli or S. aureus solution (105–106 CFU mL−1) was added to each gradient tube. The samples were incubated with bacteria for 24 h at 37 °C with shaking. The endpoint where turbidity cannot be observed corresponds to MIC. The control group added with the same amount of PBS without antibacterial materials was the negative control group. All tests were performed in triplicate.

The sample ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA (2 mg) was added to 4 mL of phosphate buffer saline (pH 7.4 or pH 5.5) containing 100 μL of E. coli and S. aureus (105–106 CFU mL−1), respectively, and the mixtures were incubated on a shaker at 37 °C with a rotation speed of 150 rpm. 100 μL of the mixtures were taken at specified time (0, 20, 40, 60, 90 and 150 min, respectively), followed inoculating on the LB solid medium. The LB solid medium were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and the numbers of viable bacteria colonies were counted. The control groups without the samples were carried out in the same method. Each test was performed in triplicate.

The sample (0.5 mg mL−1) was incubated with bacterial suspension (~104 CFU mL−1) at 37 °C for different times. The group without samples served as the blank control. Subsequently, the coverslips were washed with PBS and fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 12 h. Finally, the dehydration was sequentially performed with different concentrations of ethanol (30, 50, 70, 80, 90, 95 and 100%). The treated bacteria were dried and sputtered with gold particles. The morphological changes of bacterial cells were observed with SEM.

DCFH-DA fluorescent staining was used to evaluate intracellular ROS levels induced by nanocomposites [41]. Briefly, 300 μL S. aureus (1 × 108 CFU mL−1) was added to the each sterile vial. Next, 2 mL of DCFH-DA (10 mM) or sterile water was treated with the bacteria. The mixtures were then incubated at 37 °C for 20 min in a shaker. Subsequently, ZIF-8, ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA (500 μg mL−1) samples dispersed in PBS (pH 7.4 or pH 5.5) for 1 h, 4 h, 10 h and 20 h, respectively, or 1 mM H2O2 (positive control), sterile water (negative control) were added to the vials respectively. The total volume of each vial maintains 3 mL. The samples were cultured at 37 °C for 10 min in a shaker. Finally, the fluorescence intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The content of ROS was directly proportional to the fluorescent intensity of DCF.

Peroxidase-like activity of ZIF-8-based nanocomposites was studied by TMB colorimetric reaction [42]. Specifically, the ZIF-8-based nanocomposites (100 μg mL−1) was mixed with TMB (0.75 mM) in the acetate buffer solution (pH 5.5) to react at 25 °C for 10 min. The absorbance of the oxidized TMB was measured with a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 652 nm.

The photoreduction reaction of NBT is used to detect the steady state concentration of •O2 − [27]. ZIF-8-based nanocomposite (100 μg mL−1) was mixed with NTB (4 mM) in the PBS solution (pH = 5.5) and reacted for 30 min. NBT will be reduced to insoluble blue formazan by •O2 −. NBT was monitored at 260 nm using UV–visible spectroscopy, which indirectly assesses the level of •O2 −.

The classic reagent TA reacts with OH• to generate fluorescent TA-OH was used to detect the production of OH•, which was detected by the fluorescence spectrometer with an excitation at 315 nm [43]. ZIF-8-based nanocomposite (100 μg mL−1) was mixed with TA (4 mM) in PBS (pH = 5.5) and reacted for 40 min. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of the solution was characterized by the fluorescence spectrophotometer.

For biofilm culture, S. aureus (106 CFU mL−1) was incubated with 2 mL of 0.5% glucose-added trypsin soy broth (TSB) in the 12-well microplates. The above inoculation solution was statically incubated in an incubator at 37 °C for 48 h. After 48 h, the formed biofilms were gently washed three times with PBS for future use.

In the experimental group, the fresh TSB solution containing different nanocomposites (0.5 mg mL−1) was added into the biofilm prepared in advance, which was also incubated in each well of the 12-well microplate for 24 h. The biofilm of control group only added the same amount of TSB culture solution, continuing to incubate for 24 h. After incubation, the attached biofilm were carefully washed with PBS to remove the metabolic impurities and plankton bacteria. The morphological changes of the biofilm were observed by SEM. After 15 min of staining with AO and EB in darkness, CLSM 3D imaging was used to detect the survival rate of bacteria inside the biofilm.

In addition, the CV staining experiment also detects the biofilm destruction ability of ZIF-8-based nanocomposites. Similarly, biofilms incubated with the samples were stained with 1% CV for 15 min after washing. To remove the excess CV stain, the biofilm was gently washed 3 times with PBS. The attached CV was dissolved in absolute ethanol. For biomass analysis, the absorbance at 590 nm of the lysate was detected by a microplate reader. Each test was performed in triplicate.

The viability of NIH-3 T3 mouse fibroblast cell was measured by employing the CCK-8 assay to assess the in vitro cell cytotoxicity [45]. NIH-3 T3 cells were cultured in Eagle's medium supplemented with 10% fetal calf serum and 1% antibiotic antifungal solution at 37 °C for 24 h. Samples of different concentrations (0, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, 500 μg mL−1) were incorporated into the prepared cells and incubated for 1, 3, and 5 days. The sample-treated NIH-3 T3 cells were then incubated with 10 μL of CCK-8 solution (2 mg mL−1) for 4 h. Finally, the cell survival rate relative to the blank control group was measured on a microplate reader by characterizing the absorbance intensity of CCK-8 at 450 nm. Each experiment was repeated five times.

All statistical datas were from at least three parallel experiments and were expressed as mean ± standard deviation. According to the TTEST function in Microsoft Excel, the significance between the statistical data was evaluated by a twotailed heteroscedastic t-test. A value of p < 0.05 was considered to be significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of ZIF-8-based nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The facile one-pot approach to ZIF-8-based nanocomposites is shown in Scheme 1 . SEM images (Fig. 1 a-c) present the thermodynamically favorable truncated rhombic dodecahedral structure of the obtained ZIF-8, ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA. Thereinto, ZIF-8 exhibits a particle size of about 70 nm (Fig. 1a). The size of the ZIF-8@Lys-CD (Fig. 1b) and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA (Fig. 1c) increases to 90 nm and 120 nm, respectively. Compared with well-defined shape of ZIF-8, ZIF-8-based nanocomposites show less regular structure, implying the encapsulation of guest units within ZIF-8 host. In addition, ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA shows the rougher surface morphology, which is attributed to the addition of both Lys-CDs and FA. TEM observation (Fig. 1d-f) is applied for further exploring the structure of obtained particles. Comparing with the images of ZIF-8 (Fig. 1d), small sesame-shaped white spots appeared inside ZIF-8@Lys-CD (Fig. 1e), which is probably considered to be encapsulated Lys-CD with the size of 3–8 nm (Fig. S1). For ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA (Fig. 1f), the color of nano-particles is significantly darker, which may be due to the presence of small FA molecules. The hydrodynamic diameter of obtained nanocomposites through DLS analysis are correlated with SEM and TEM results. As shown in Fig. 1h-i, the average particle sizes are about 350 nm for ZIF-8, 500 nm for ZIF-8@Lys-CD, and 800 nm for ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA, respectively. The largely higher particle size observed in the DLS analysis is due to the swelling of NMOFs in water medium. However, the trend of change is well consistent with the results observed by TEM and SEM. All these results indicate clearly that the conjugation of Lys-CDs and FA does not interrupt the formation of NMOFs.

The crystal structures of the obtained samples have been determined by X-ray power diffraction technique (XRD). From Fig. 2 a, ZIF-8 particles are of high crystallinity for the presence of sharp characteristic diffraction peaks located at 2θ = 7.75°, 10.78°, 13.18°, 15.20°, 16.93°, 18.56°, 22.5°, 24.94°, 27.14°, 30.05°, 32.88°, which coincides well with the plane indices of (011), (002), (112), (022), (013), (222), (114), (233), (134), (044), (235) respectively [46]. However, the corresponding diffraction peaks of ZIF-8@Lys-CD exhibit an obvious deviation to lower values indicating an increase in the crystal space according to the Bragg's Law. This increase in interplanar distance could be attributed to the coordination of Lys-CDs inside ZIF-8. After co-loading Lys-CDs and FA, it's worth noting that XRD pattern of ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA corresponds nearly with that of the standard ZIF-8, demonstrating the formation of similar crystal octahedrons to pure ZIF-8. It is determined that Lys-CDs possesses positive charge (Fig. S2). So we hypothesize that this interesting result may be due to the incorporation of the negatively charged FA on the surface of Lys-CDs nanoparticles with cationic features by electrostatic interaction, thereby inhibiting the coordination of the Lys-CDs with the central ions in ZIF-8.

To clearly elucidate the structure of ZIF-8-based nanocomposites, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of ZIF-8, ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA have been recorded (Fig. 2b). In case of ZIF-8@Lys-CD, the original peak of Lys-CDs at 1666 cm−1 for the CO stretching vibration in the amide I region appears, which also shows the characteristic peak of ZIF-8. The bands in region of 944–1305 cm−1 and band at 1423 cm−1 correspond to the imidazole ring, and the band at 443 cm−1 is attributed the ZnN stretch mode [47], suggesting the successful incorporation of Lys-CDs to the ZIF-8. However, the stretching vibration peak of the ZnN bond is significantly weakened and blue-shifted from 423 cm−1 to 443 cm−1, which suggests that Lys-CDs successfully embedded in ZIF-8 by the interaction of the Zn2+ with nitrogen-containing group residues on Lys-CDs surface. For ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA, the characteristic band at 1337 cm−1 corresponds to the phenyl ring of FA, which indicates that the FA molecule exists inside ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA. Furthermore, the peak corresponding to the ZnN covalent bond in the ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA framework is obvious strengthened and has only slightly blue-shifted 423 cm−1 to 428 cm−1. This may be due to the fact that FA inhibited the coordination of zinc ion and Lys-CDs by electrostatic adsorption on the surface of Lys-CDs. This result is well in accordance with that of XRD. To further certify the of the incorporation mechanism of Lys-CDs and FA in ZIF-8, UV–vis absorption was measured. As depicted in Fig. 2c, ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA shows the broad absorption with multiple bands. It is known that, in UV–vis spectrum of single Lys-CDs (Fig. 2d), the weakened band at 250 nm ~ 300 nm is the intrinsic absorption of the aromatic sp. [2] hybrid region, which is attributed to π → π* of the carbon core, including CC, CN [48]. The absorbance at band 310 nm ~ 350 nm, typically, is ascribed to the surface and edge defects of the Lys-CDs (n → π*) [49]. However, after being embedded in ZIF-8@Lys-CD (Fig. 2c), the maximum absorption peak of Lys-CDs has shifted from 330 nm to 310 nm. For clarify this phenomenon, the UV–vis spectrum of Lys-CDs and Zn2+ mixture is measured, and substantially the same result of blue shifted absorption is obtained (Fig. 2d). The results might be related to the coordination between Zn2+ and Lys-CDs. Differently, mixing FA with zinc ions, the characteristic peak of mixture is similar to the absorption behavior of free FA (Fig. 2e). Similarly, the band of FA in the spectrum of ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA (Fig. 2c) corresponds to pure FA, indicating that FA is not involved in coordination. Compared with ZIF-8@Lys-CD, the blue shift of Lys-CDs in ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA has been weakened, which is well in accordance with that of XRD and FTIR tests.

The TGA test is used to understand the thermal stability of ZIF-8-based nanocomposites and the approximate content of antibacterial ingredients. As shown in Fig. 2f, ZIF-8-based nanocomposites are still thermally stable at a temperature of 300 °C. The thermal stability of ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA doped with antibacterial agent is slightly worse than that of pure ZIF-8, which may be as the cavity of the ZIF frameworks are filled with Lys-CDs and FA. In addition, comparing the TGA curves of ZIF-8@Lys-CD and pure Lys-CDs, approximately 15.4% of Lys-CDs is present in the ZIF-8@Lys-CD framework. Furthermore, compared to ZIF-8@Lys-CD, it is not difficult to find that the weight percent of Lys-CD and FA in ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA are 35.7% totally.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial properties and mechanism of ZIF-8-based nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: MIC test is used to assess the sensitivity of E. coli and S. aureus to synthetic ZIF-8-based nanocomposites in the neutral or acidic environments. The antibacterial results are presented in Table 1 . The MIC value of both strains co-cultured with pure ZIF-8 at pH 7.4 is as high as 500 μg mL−1. However, the growth of S. aureus and E. coli is successfully inhibited when the ZIF-8 concentration was 125 μg mL−1 and 250 μg mL−1 in pH 5.5 medium, respectively. The MIC values of ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA against S. aureus and E. coli are the same at pH 7.4, which are 250 μg mL−1 and 500 μg mL−1, respectively. ZIF-8@Lys-CD significantly inhibited the growth of S. aureus and E. coli at pH 5.5 showing MIC values of 62.5 μg mL−1 and 125 μg mL−1, severally. In addition, in the acidic environments, S. aureus and E. coli become more sensitive to ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA with MIC values of 32 μg mL−1 and 62.5 μg mL−1 at pH 5.5, and 62.5 μg mL−1 and 125 μg mL−1 at pH 6.5, respectively. Here, by comparing the MIC values of ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA, it is found that FA plays an important role in the antimicrobial efficiency of ZIF-8-based nanocomposites in addition to Lys-CDs, although FA itself does not have obvious antibacterial ability (Table S1). Along with the targeting of FA, ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA is easily recognized by the cell surface receptors of the strain and successfully inhibits the growth of bacteria during the binding process with bacteria.

The antibacterial efficiency of ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA is further studied by time-dynamic bactericidal assay. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the bacterial killing activity displays time dependence. And, similarly, ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA exhibits enhanced antibacterial activity under acid environment, which is expected to be more effective for combating the accumulation of bacteria. Next, the surface morphological changes of bacteria during contact with ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA nanocomposites are observed. It is clearly observed that the smooth surface of the intact membrane structure of the bacteria (Fig. 3(b) a, d) is destroyed with accompanying the cracks and shrinkage. And the surface of bacteria cells becomes rough with a small gap causing the membrane permeabilization and bacteria collapse (Fig. 3(b) b, e). As the co-cultivation time is extended, the bacteria are irreversibly destroyed. Eventually, extravasation of intracellular contents (Fig. 3(b) c, f) results in the bacterial death.

To investigate the effect of pH on the antibacterial ability of ZIF-8-based nanocomposites, the degradation rate is investigated at different pH values. Because the coordination between zinc ions and 2-methylimidazolate is acid-sensitive, ZIF-8 crystals are stable in the neutral environment but decompose under acidic conditions to release the drug [21,24,50]. Therefore, after the successful construction of ZIF-8-based nanocomposites has been verified, the acid-triggered decomposition is successively investigated. The antibacterial agents expose behavior of ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA is monitored via measuring the UV–Vis absorption of Lys-CDs and FA in PBS at pH 5.5 and 7.4, mimicking the pH levels present in bacteria accumulation site and normal environment, respectively. As represented in Fig. 4 (a, b), ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA in PBS buffer solutions reveal parallel sustained expose behaviors of Lys-CDs. At pH 7.4 for 30 h, only 22.7% and 26.3% of Lys-CDs is exposed from the drug carrier (ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA) due to the stability of the ZIF-8-based nanocomposites in the neutral condition. In contrast, the final cumulative amounts of Lys-CDs from ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA at pH 5.5 reach 51.7% and 60.2%, respectively, indicating that the acid medium is a favorable condition for the dissociation of the ZIF-8 structure and promotes Lys-CDs exposure. It is reported that the pH sensitivity of the coordination bonds in ZIF-8 results from the protonation of the ligand under acidic conditions [23,51]. In view of these properties, ZIF-8 can be used as an antibacterial substance carrier and pH-trigger to enhance the antibacterial activity of Lys-CDs against bacteria and reduce side effects. And furthermore, FA is a small molecule which is more easily released from the ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA framework than the Lys-CDs. Another potential reason lies in the better hydrophilicity of FA under acidic conditions compared to that under neutral conditions. At the end, the cumulative amount of FA release at pH 5.5 reaches a plateau with 88% exposure within 30 h (Fig. 4c). In addition, TEM results show the morphological changes during the disintegration of ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA at pH 5.5 with concomitant exposure of Lys-CDs and FA (Fig. 4d-f). Besides, the release of Zn2+ has also been determined (Fig. S6). The results show that the concentration of released Zn2+ increases with time, and the acid medium promotes Zn2+ exposure. Anyway, there is no doubt that the rapid disintegration of ZIF-8-based nanocomposites at pH 5.5 would dramatically improve the delivery efficacy locating in the acidic environment of bacterial deposition.

It is reported that ROS may be a common mechanism component of cell death induced by bactericidals [52–54]. In order to explore the bacterial killing mechanism of ZIF-8-based nanocomposites, the relative content of ROS in bacteria cells is detected after different samples treatments. DCFH-DA is used as a ROS indicator to assess intracellular ROS levels of bacteria treated with ZIF-8-based nanocomposites. In this experiment, samples are first dispersed in neutral and acidic media (pH 7.4 and pH 5.5) for different times (1 h, 4 h, 10, 20 h), and finally contacted with S. aureus and indicator. The results show that S. aureus treated with ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA under different conditions generate the most intracellular DCF+, indicating the most effective ROS production (Fig. 5 , Fig. S3). In addition, each of the ZIF-8-based nanocomposites is previously immersed in pH 7.4 and pH 5.5 PBS for a period of time, respectively. By comparison, it is found that ZIF-8-based nanocomposites, especially ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA, can produce more ROS in bacterial cells after soaking in acidic PBS (Fig. 5a). Finally, the increasing ROS produced inside the bacterial cells is accompanied by prolonged soaking time. It is speculated that these results may be due to the fact that the ZIF-8-based framework is easily disintegrated under acidic conditions, so that metal ions, Lys-CDs and FA are freely exposed. Further, it is speculated that an increase in ROS within the bacteria may be the result of a combination of these free naked components.

In order to get a deeper insight into the process of generating ROS by ZIF-8-based nanocomposites, the same method is used to determine the relative content of ROS inside the bacteria after contact with the raw materials of different combinations. Specifically, the raw materials are first dispersed in PBS of pH 5.5 for 1 h, then contacted with bacteria separately. Finally, the relative content of ROS in different groups is compared by DCFH-DA fluorescence test. As can be seen from Fig. 5b, the independent Zn2+, Lys-CDs, and ZIF-8 produce the insignificant amount of ROS. FA, as an antioxidant, even inhibits the production of ROS. In contrast, the combination of ZIF-8 with Lys-CDs or ZIF-8 with FA dispersed in PBS of pH 5.5 for 1 h, eventually detect an obvious increase in intracellular ROS content. In particular, when ZIF-8, Lys-CDs and FA are mixed together simultaneously, the amount of intracellular ROS is drastically increased. Based on this study, we believe that the reason for the large amount of ROS in the bacterial cells treated by ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA is the cumulative exposure of Lys-CDs and FA under the stimulation of acid conditions. However, previous studies have shown that FA acts as very potent antioxidants that can efficiently scavenge ROS [55,56]. For understanding this counterintuitive result, we speculate the possible antibacterial mechanism. ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA releases FA easily, which can bind to the folate receptor on the surface of bacterial cells. Then, folate receptor-mediated endocytosis causes Lys-CDs and the unstable ZIF framework to enter the interior of the bacteria, making a significant contribution to ROS production. Finally, a large amount of intracellular ROS destroys the integrity of the cell membrane and disrupts the normal physiological activities of the bacteria through oxidative stress [57]. Compared to ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA, the amount of intracellular ROS produced by the contact of ZIF-8@Lys-CD with bacteria is drastically reduced due to the absence of FA (Fig. 5a).

More specifically, the porous interface of the ZIF-8 series of materials provides many active sites and facilitates mass transfer in the catalytic process, which makes ZIF-8-based nanocomposites exhibit peroxidase (POD)-like activity [58]. Here the POD-like activity of ZIF-8-based nanocomposites at pH 5.5 is investigated. The catalytic efficiency of H2O2 is confirmed by the colorimetric reaction of the oxidized substrate TMB. In the presence of H2O2, TMB solution will turn blue after adding the substance with POD-like activity, which is related to the formation of radical cations of the TMB (TMB+) charge transfer complex (CTC) and shows the UV–Vis absorption at 651 nm (Fig. 6 a) [59]. In comparison, as-prepared ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA exhibit higher catalytic efficiency than ZIF-8 and ZIF-8@Lys-CD as shown in the Fig. 6b. This result provides evidence that ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA possesses better peroxidase-like catalytic activity. To further test the ability of ZIF-8-based nanocomposites in ROS regulation, we investigate the important free radical constituents of reactive oxygen species, such as •O2 − and •OH. First, •O2 − has been quantified by employing NBT reduction method [60]. As a result of the reduction by •O2 − the yellow NBT becomes blue formazan, which has been used to detect •O2 − in nanoenzyme catalysis (Fig. 6c). As seen in Fig. 6d, the absorption spectra of NBT reacted with each ZIF-8-based nanocomposite show that ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA produces •O2 − with better efficiency than ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8. Similarly, to detect •OH, the classic TA is used as a fluorescent indicator to react with •OH to generate fluorescent TA-OH (Fig. 6e), which is monitored by a fluorescence spectrometer under excitation at 315 nm [61]. The experiments are conducted at pH 5.5. As shown in Fig. 6f, at the same concentrations, ZIF-8, ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA are added to the TA solution, respectively. By contrast, the fluorescence intensity of the TA solution with ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA is significantly higher than the other two groups, which demonstrated that much more •OH is produced than ZIF-8 and ZIF-8@Lys-CD. Therefore, the higher content of hydroxyl radical makes ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA exhibit better antibacterial properties since the •OH possesses more potent oxidizability.

Unlike planktonic bacteria cells, bacterial biofilms have high intrinsic resistance to antibacterial agents, and it is a significant problematic challenge to overcome them in a eutrophic environment where bacteria accumulate [62]. Several studies have reported that ROS are responsible for inducing bacteria death, decomposing the extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) matrix and possibly inhibiting biofilm development. Through the above results, it has been confirmed that ZIF-8-based nanocomposites have the unique dissociation property and a distinct antibacterial efficiency in acid environment, which made it interesting as a potential ROS generator for disrupting biofilms. To investigate the antibiofilm activity, the morphology and structural changes of mature S. aureus biofilms are observed by SEM after co-cultivation with the ZIF-8-based nanocomposites (Fig. 7 a-h). In the untreated group, the biofilms with normal structure are clearly visible with dense and firm clusters of internal bacteria (Fig. 7a, e). Under the same growth conditions but treated with ZIF-8, ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA, biofilms show varying degrees of structural change. In the ZIF-8 experimental group (Fig. 7b, f), the bacterial biofilm on the micro-slider surfaces is reduced where a small pile rather than a whole piece of aggregated colonies is observed, which means the biofilms start to collapse. The SEM image of the ZIF-8@Lys-CD treated sample (Fig. 7c, g) clearly exhibit that the colonies are spread out in a thin layer and there are no more colonies like hills. The morphology of treated bacteria shows compromised and the inconsistency of rough cell surface, and cellular contents leaking out are visible. Furthermore, the targeting of FA improved the antibacterial and antibiofilm effects of the ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA. Fig. 7d displays more regions with only single bacteria adhered or even free of bacteria. In Fig. 7h, it can be observed that most of the bacterial surface is bound with some nanoparticles. Probably due to the targeting of FA, ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA adhere to the bacterial cell membrane, which facilitates the penetration of the nanoparticles into the interior of the bacteria.

To further understand the antibiofilm behaviors and reveal changes in the S. aureus cell viability inside biofilms treated with prepared nanocomposites, CLSM 3D images are collected and analyzed (Fig. 7i-l). A large number of green clusters of live bacteria are observed in untreated control (Fig. 7i), which indicates an intact and dense biofilm structure with multiple layers of almost entirely of live bacteria. With treatment of ZIF-8 (Fig. 7j), only a few S. aureus on the surface of the thick biofilm is killed, whereas the structure of biofilm seems to be compact. What's more, lots of viable bacteria in deeper biofilm layers are still packed by EPS. It is due to nanocomposites with insufficient ROS levels which cannot break through the solid network of matrix in biofilms. For the ZIF-8@Lys-CDs treated samples, green fluorescence intensity is significantly decreased and reddish brown fluorescing cells drastically increase (Fig. 7k), which indicates the effect of Lys-CDs in killing the cells and disrupting the biofilm structure. As mentioned above, the ZIF-8 framework is degraded in the acid environment of bacterial metabolism, thereby exposing Lys-CDs to the surrounding environments. As we know, Lys-CDs with cationic features leads to the Lys-CDs having a strong destruction to the bacterial membrane (membrane potential ca. −100 to −150 mV) [63]. In addition, intracellular ROS induced by Lys-CDs decomposes stubborn EPS and promotes the entry of antibacterial agents into the biofilms, thereby inactivating bacteria deep in the biofilms. Precisely, in the CLSM images of ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA treatment, biofilms consist of less biomass and only small numbers of live cell clusters exist, revealing biofilms appear nearly disrupted (Fig. 7l). We infer that, with the introducing of FA, ZIF-8-based nanocomposites produce more active ROS in acid environment and exhibit a specific targeting effect to bacterial biofilms, resulting in the enhanced antibiofilm property. Conclusively, both SEM and CLSM analysis show low density of S. aureus treated with ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA, thereby suggests the effective antibiofilm property of ZIF-8 framework embedded with Lys-CDs or Lys-CD/FA.

Crystal violet staining results (Fig. S4) quantitatively demonstrate that the efficiency of biofilm destruction increases significantly as Lys-CDs or both Lys-CDs and FA are encapsulated in the ZIF-8 framework. These results are consistent with SEM and CLSM observations and further confirm that the prepared ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA have obvious effects on the destruction of mature biofilms.

In vitro cell viabilities are determined to evaluate the effect of ZIF-8-based nanocomposites on mammalian cells by a CCK-8 assay. Fig. 8 shows viability of NIH-3 T3 fibroblast cells after being incubated with as prepared nanocomposites for 1 day, 3 day and 5 day respectively. The results reveal that ZIF-8@Lys-CD is obviously more biocompatible than bare ZIF-8, exhibiting cell viability of nearly 100% even at a high concentration of 250 μg mL−1 (Fig. 8b). With the adding of FA, the cell viability of ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA is almost the same as that of bare ZIF-8 (Fig. 8c), which can be attributed to the sharply increased ROS level. Contemporarily, ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA show the harmlessness to NIH-3 T3 fibroblast cell at concentrations up to 125 μg mL−1 (2–4 fold MIC for tested bacteria in bacteria-secreted acid environment), suggesting the tolerable cytotoxicity of ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA toward normal mammal cells.

In summary, we have developed NMOFs encapsulating Lys-CDs or Lys-CD/FA in a one-pot synthesis. ZIF-8@Lys-CD has effective antibacterial abilities, and can also play a certain destructive effect on mature biofilms. More attractively, the biocompatibility of ZIF-8@Lys-CD is significantly higher than that of ZIF-8. For ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA, the targeting of FA can induce a large amount of ROS in the bacterial cells. Therefore, ZIF-8@Lys-CD@FA is superior to ZIF-8@Lys-CD and ZIF-8 in its ability to disrupt bacteria and destroy mature biofilms. In addition, the instability of the ZIF-8 framework in the acid environment plays a crucial role throughout the antimicrobial process. This rational design has been successfully used to develop an acid-activated ROS generator. With these subtle properties, the FA targeting ZIF-8-based nanocomposites developed with carbon dots in our research may have a potential in antimicrobial and antibiofilm use.
